title: HCI Shared Storage I/O Latency (Cluster, Host, VM, Disk Group)
exclusions: This UC is not a replacement for UC-19.2.27 free-space, slack, or rebuild headroom, UC-19.2.7 Controller VM process availability, UC-19.2.17 disk or cache component health, or UC-19.2.9 resync and rebuild time after failure. It does not monitor guest OS filesystem fullness, database transaction log free space, or network oversubscription except where those show up as storage latency. It does not, by itself, prove application-level SQL or Oracle p95; add APM (UC-19.1.x) when the database is off-cluster. CIM `Performance.Storage` is optional; if your TA mapping is incomplete, stay on the raw vendor sourcetypes here.
evidence: Saved search (for example `ss_uc_19_2_3_hci_io_latency_p95_v1`) scheduled every three to five minutes, CSV or PDF of the `table` output in `index=evidence` on each P1, `lookups/latency_baseline.csv` in version control, and a screenshot of the vSAN or Prism performance tab for a sampled `critical` row linked in the ticket.
dataSources: PRIMARY: `(index=vmware OR index=hci) sourcetype=vmware:vsan:perfquery` from a custom HEC line per entity per sample from the vCenter `/api/.../vsan/.../perf-query` (or your wrapped path) for entities `cluster-domclient:*`, `host-domclient:*`, `disk-group:*`, and `vm:*` with `latency_avg_read_ms`, `latency_avg_write_ms`, `latency_max_read_ms`, `latency_max_write_ms`, `iops_read`, `iops_write`, and optional `congestion` / `outstanding_io` / `cache_hit_rate_pct` when your poller adds them. PRIMARY (Nutanix): `nutanix:cluster:metrics` and `nutanix:host:metrics` from `Splunk_TA_nutanix` (5076) with `controller_avg_io_latency_usecs` and the split read and write `*_usecs` fields, plus `controller_num_iops`. PRIMARY (Cisco HyperFlex): `cisco:hyperflex:cluster_perf` and `cisco:hyperflex:vm_perf` with `latency_us` and `iops` or `read_iops`/`write_iops`. OPTIONAL: `azure_stack_hci:storage_perf` (PowerShell to HEC) with `Latency`, `IOPS`, `InitiatorName`, `BandwidthMBps` when present. Lookups: `lookups/latency_baseline.csv` keyed on `cluster_name` to supply tier and notification metadata.
app: Custom HEC pollers (vCenter perf-query, Cisco HX REST) plus `Splunk_TA_nutanix` ([Splunkbase 5076](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5076)). Optional: Splunk Add-on for VMware ([Splunkbase 3215](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3215)) for CIM `Performance.*` or event gaps; optional `Splunk ITSI` (see `premiumApps`) for service-level KPIs.
spl:
`comment("UC-19.2.3 — HCI shared storage I/O read/write latency (p95, max) against per-cluster baselines. SOURCES: vmware:vsan:perfquery, nutanix:cluster:metrics, nutanix:host:metrics, cisco:hyperflex:cluster_perf, cisco:hyperflex:vm_perf, optional azure_stack_hci:storage_perf. earliest=-30m@m; change freeze demotes to ok except when severity is critical (including read_max_ms>100 OR write_max_ms>100).")`
| multisearch [
    search (index=vmware OR index=hci) sourcetype="vmware:vsan:perfquery" earliest=-30m@m
    | eval vendor="vmware"
    | eval cluster_name=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, cluster, cluster_id, clusterId, ""))
    | eval host_name=toString(coalesce(host_name, hostName, esxi_host, esxiHost, ""))
    | eval entity=lower(toString(coalesce(entity, entity_id, entityId, "")))
    | eval scope=case(like(entity, "cluster-domclient%"), "cluster", like(entity, "host-domclient%"), "host", like(entity, "disk-group%"), "disk_group", match(entity, "^vm:"), "vm", 1=1, "other")
    | eval latency_avg_read_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_avg_read_ms, latencyAvgReadMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval latency_avg_write_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_avg_write_ms, latencyAvgWriteMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval latency_max_read_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_max_read_ms, latencyMaxReadMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval latency_max_write_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_max_write_ms, latencyMaxWriteMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval iops_r=tonumber(toString(coalesce(iops_read, iopsRead, "0")), 10)
    | eval iops_w=tonumber(toString(coalesce(iops_write, iopsWrite, "0")), 10)
    | eval iops_total=iops_r+iops_w
    | table _time, cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope, latency_avg_read_ms, latency_avg_write_ms, latency_max_read_ms, latency_max_write_ms, iops_total
] [
    search (index=hci OR index=nutanix) (sourcetype="nutanix:cluster:metrics" OR sourcetype="nutanix:host:metrics") earliest=-30m@m
    | eval vendor="nutanix"
    | eval cluster_name=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name, ""))
    | eval host_name=toString(coalesce(host_name, hostName, node_name, nodeName, ""))
    | eval scope=if(sourcetype="nutanix:host:metrics", "host", "cluster")
    | eval lr=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_read_io_latency_usecs, controllerAvgReadIoLatencyUsecs, "0")), 10)/1000.0
    | eval lw=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_write_io_latency_usecs, controllerAvgWriteIoLatencyUsecs, "0")), 10)/1000.0
    | eval lcomb=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_io_latency_usecs, controllerAvgIoLatencyUsecs, "0")), 10)/1000.0
    | eval latency_avg_read_ms=if((lr>0), lr, lcomb)
    | eval latency_avg_write_ms=if((lw>0), lw, lcomb)
    | eval latency_max_read_ms=latency_avg_read_ms
    | eval latency_max_write_ms=latency_avg_write_ms
    | eval iops_total=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_num_iops, controllerNumIops, "0")), 10)
    | table _time, cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope, latency_avg_read_ms, latency_avg_write_ms, latency_max_read_ms, latency_max_write_ms, iops_total
] [
    search (index=hci OR index=cisco) (sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster_perf" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:vm_perf") earliest=-30m@m
    | eval vendor="cisco"
    | eval cluster_name=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, cluster_id, clusterId, ""))
    | eval host_name=toString(coalesce(host_name, hostName, vm_name, vmName, ""))
    | eval scope=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:vm_perf", "vm", "cluster")
    | eval lat_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_ms, "0")), 10)
    | eval lat_us=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_us, latencyUs, "0")), 10)
    | eval base_ms=if((lat_ms>0), lat_ms, (lat_us/1000.0))
    | eval read_i=tonumber(toString(coalesce(read_iops, readIops, "0")), 10)
    | eval write_i=tonumber(toString(coalesce(write_iops, writeIops, "0")), 10)
    | eval all_i=tonumber(toString(coalesce(iops, "0")), 10)
    | eval iops_total=if((all_i>0), all_i, (read_i+write_i))
    | eval latency_avg_read_ms=base_ms
    | eval latency_avg_write_ms=base_ms
    | eval latency_max_read_ms=base_ms
    | eval latency_max_write_ms=base_ms
    | table _time, cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope, latency_avg_read_ms, latency_avg_write_ms, latency_max_read_ms, latency_max_write_ms, iops_total
] [
    search (index=hci OR index=ashci) sourcetype="azure_stack_hci:storage_perf" earliest=-30m@m
    | eval vendor="ashci"
    | eval cluster_name=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, ClusterName, ""))
    | eval host_name=toString(coalesce(InitiatorName, initiatorName, host_name, hostName, ""))
    | eval scope="vm"
    | eval latency_avg_read_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(Latency, latency, "0")), 10)
    | eval latency_avg_write_ms=latency_avg_read_ms
    | eval latency_max_read_ms=latency_avg_read_ms
    | eval latency_max_write_ms=latency_avg_read_ms
    | eval iops_total=tonumber(toString(coalesce(IOPS, iops, "0")), 10)
    | table _time, cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope, latency_avg_read_ms, latency_avg_write_ms, latency_max_read_ms, latency_max_write_ms, iops_total
]
| where len(cluster_name)>0
| stats perc95(latency_avg_read_ms) AS read_p95_ms perc95(latency_avg_write_ms) AS write_p95_ms max(latency_max_read_ms) AS read_max_ms max(latency_max_write_ms) AS write_max_ms sum(iops_total) AS iops_total max(_time) AS last_event BY cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope
| lookup local=true latency_baseline cluster_name OUTPUTNEW tier_type, read_threshold_ms, write_threshold_ms, business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until, criticality_class
| eval read_base=case(tier_type="all_flash" OR match(lower(tostring(tier_type)), "all.flash"), 1, tier_type="nvme" OR match(lower(tostring(tier_type)), "nvme"), 0.5, 1=1, 5)
| eval write_base=case(tier_type="all_flash" OR match(lower(tostring(tier_type)), "all.flash"), 2, tier_type="nvme" OR match(lower(tostring(tier_type)), "nvme"), 1, 1=1, 10)
| eval read_threshold_ms=if((isnotnull(read_threshold_ms) AND (read_threshold_ms>0)), read_threshold_ms, read_base)
| eval write_threshold_ms=if((isnotnull(write_threshold_ms) AND (write_threshold_ms>0)), write_threshold_ms, write_base)
| eval tier_guess=if((read_threshold_ms<=1) AND (write_threshold_ms<=2), "likely_all_flash_or_nvme", "likely_hybrid_or_mixed")
| eval latency_drift_pct=if((read_threshold_ms>0), round(((read_p95_ms - read_threshold_ms) / read_threshold_ms) * 100, 1), null())
| join type=left max=0 cluster_name [
    search (index=vmware OR index=hci OR index=nutanix) (sourcetype="vmware:vsan:perfquery" OR sourcetype="nutanix:cluster:metrics" OR sourcetype="nutanix:host:metrics") earliest=-25h@h latest=-30m@m
    | eval c=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, cluster, name, ""))
    | eval n_r_us=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_read_io_latency_usecs, "0")), 10)
    | eval n_w_us=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_write_io_latency_usecs, "0")), 10)
    | eval n_c_us=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_io_latency_usecs, "0")), 10)
    | eval v_r=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_avg_read_ms, latencyAvgReadMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval v_w=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_avg_write_ms, latencyAvgWriteMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval r_t=if(n_r_us>0, n_r_us/1000, if(n_c_us>0, n_c_us/1000, v_r))
    | eval w_t=if(n_w_us>0, n_w_us/1000, if(n_c_us>0, n_c_us/1000, v_w))
    | where len(c)>0
    | stats perc95(r_t) AS read_p95_24h perc95(w_t) AS write_p95_24h BY c
    | rename c AS cluster_name
]
| eval trend_up=if((isnotnull(read_p95_24h) OR isnotnull(write_p95_24h)) AND (read_p95_ms <= read_threshold_ms) AND (write_p95_ms <= write_threshold_ms) AND (((read_p95_24h>0) AND (read_p95_ms > 1.1*read_p95_24h)) OR ((write_p95_24h>0) AND (write_p95_ms > 1.1*write_p95_24h))), 1, 0)
| eval severity_bucket=case((read_p95_ms > 4*read_threshold_ms) OR (write_p95_ms > 4*write_threshold_ms) OR (read_max_ms > 100) OR (write_max_ms > 100), "critical", (read_p95_ms > 2*read_threshold_ms) OR (write_p95_ms > 2*write_threshold_ms), "high", (read_p95_ms > read_threshold_ms) OR (write_p95_ms > write_threshold_ms), "medium", (trend_up=1), "low", 1=1, "ok")
| eval ft=coalesce(strptime(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"), strptime(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%d"))
| eval freeze_active=if((isnotnull(change_freeze_until) AND (len(trim(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, ""))))>0) AND (now() < coalesce(ft, 0))), 1, 0)
| eval severity_bucket=if((freeze_active=1) AND (severity_bucket!="critical"), "ok", severity_bucket)
| eval sev_order=case(severity_bucket="critical", 1, severity_bucket="high", 2, severity_bucket="medium", 3, severity_bucket="low", 4, 1=1, 5)
| sort 0 sev_order, -latency_drift_pct, cluster_name, scope, host_name
| table last_event, cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope, tier_type, tier_guess, iops_total, read_p95_ms, write_p95_ms, read_max_ms, write_max_ms, read_threshold_ms, write_threshold_ms, read_p95_24h, write_p95_24h, trend_up, latency_drift_pct, severity_bucket, freeze_active, business_service, owner_email, criticality_class
description: Tracks vSAN, Nutanix, HyperFlex, and optional Azure Stack HCI storage read and write latency at the cluster, host or node, per-VM, and per-disk-group scopes using millisecond-normalized time series, compares the last thirty minutes of p95 and max latency to per-cluster `tier_type` SLOs from a `latency_baseline` lookup with sensible hybrid and all-flash fallbacks, and classifies a five-bucket `severity_bucket` with freeze-aware routing so a storage hot spot is visible before a capacity (UC-19.2.27) or pure health (UC-19.2.17) alert would fire.
value: Applications slow when each I/O waits, not when a headline IOPS graph looks busy. A shared HCI tier can look healthy in aggregate while one VM, one node, or one disk group drags p95 and max latency into a band where SQL, VDI, or file services breach their written SLO. This use case makes that separation explicit: the same cluster can be green for space while red for latency during cache miss, controller pressure, or east-west congestion. A single alert row that already carries the owner, the business service, a drift number against a frozen baseline, and a short thirty minute window is enough for the storage team to open the right host or VM in the console without mistaking a Controller VM process outage (UC-19.2.7) for a data-path stall.
implementation: Install `Splunk_TA_nutanix` and deploy HEC pollers for `vmware:vsan:perfquery`, `cisco:hyperflex:cluster_perf` / `cisco:hyperflex:vm_perf`, and optional `azure_stack_hci:storage_perf` with props for JSON, corporate TLS, and thirty- to sixty-second sample intervals as described in Step 1. Place `lookups/latency_baseline.csv` with `transforms.conf` in the same app as the saved search. Schedule the search every three to five minutes with `earliest=-30m@m`, set alert actions and throttle keys on `cluster_name`, `host_name`, and `scope` as in Step 4.
visualization: Row one: two single value tiles (clusters with read p95 above twice the read threshold, clusters with `severity_bucket` critical). Row two: sortable table of the search output. Row three: timechart of read and write p95 by `cluster_name`. Row four: scatter of `iops_total` versus `read_p95_ms` colored by `tier_type` to spot a saturation curve.

references.title: VMware vSphere and vSAN REST (performance and monitoring)
Cisco HyperFlex Data Platform administration (context for REST and HX Connect performance)
Splunk — Use the HTTP Event Collector

knownFalsePositives: A short burst of read latency while VMware Storage DRS or vSAN rebalancing runs can move p95 for only a few minutes; correlation with a change ticket and with UC-19.2.9 resync is expected. A Nutanix Curator or MapReduce heavy window can add controller time without a guest problem; use Prism timestamps before paging. A single VM doing a Veeam or SQL backup can spike max latency while the cluster stays inside capacity SLO, which is a workload signal, not a false alarm for this UC, but you may route those hosts to a backup queue. Annual firmware or hypervisor rolling upgrades, Cisco Intersight–driven updates, and Nutanix rolling reboots can create predictable spikes; align `change_freeze_until` in the lookup. NVMe and all-flash clusters naturally sit at sub‑millisecond reads during idle periods; a jump from 0.2 to 0.6 ms is normal variance, not a page, unless the tier and application SLO say otherwise.

premiumApps.name: Splunk ITSI
premiumApps.note: optional: glass tables and per-service KPIs for p95 and drift

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites

This use case is about runtime storage latency, not vSAN or Nutanix free-space math (sibling UC-19.2.27), not Controller VM process availability (UC-19.2.7), not disk group component health (UC-19.2.17), and not resync or rebuild progress after failure (UC-19.2.9). It answers whether the storage tier is the reason applications feel slow right now.

IOPS alone misleads. A cluster that reports one hundred thousand IOPS at fifty milliseconds of latency is in trouble; the same throughput at one millisecond is often healthy. Latency is the customer-observable signal: it is what a database or file server perceives when each I/O waits at the array, the controller VM, the network, or a remote mirror.

You need to reason about four latencies in parallel, even if you roll them up in dashboards. Per-VM latency is what the app owner feels as “SQL is slow” or “the share is stuttering.” Per-host or per-node latency tells you a NIC, HBA, driver, or CVM/SCVM pressure problem before the whole estate looks bad. Per-disk-group or per-tier slice tells you a cache or capacity leg is hot, including flash cache miss storms on hybrid nodes. Per-cluster is the first screen for a multi-tenant SLO, but a green cluster with one VM at two hundred milliseconds can still be a red incident for the business that owns that VM, which is why max latency matters as much as an average. If one outlier drives max while the p95 is fine, the search still surfaces the worst case in read_max_ms and write_max_ms.

In HCI, every I/O may traverse the local hypervisor vSAN or Nutanix stack, the controller or storage service VM, the east-west network, a peer node, and a flash or spinning tier. A fault or saturation at any hop shows up as latency first. Cache hits on flash can sit below a millisecond; a cache miss to capacity disks can be five to fifteen milliseconds on hybrid; a controller under CPU pressure or a saturated link can add tens of milliseconds without changing a headline IOPS number.

Nutanix and some REST paths expose microsecond integers. The Splunk side must coalesce those fields and divide by one thousand to land in milliseconds next to vSAN and HyperFlex times that are already in milliseconds. A mismatch here looks like a four-orders problem in dashboards.

Tier guidance you should publish next to the alert text: hybrid with SSD cache and HDD capacity — treat read under five ms and write under ten ms as acceptable; write over thirty ms is often symptomatic. All-flash — read under one ms and write under two ms; read over five ms or write over ten ms is symptomatic. NVMe or very fast all-flash — read under half a millisecond and write under one; anything above three ms should get attention. Application-aware: structured databases and latency-sensitive OLTP often want p95 read under about five ms; Microsoft Exchange and general file services often tolerate up to about twenty ms for some workloads; big analytics and batch can tolerate up to about fifty ms if that is the written business rule.

Finally, the lookup `latency_baseline` (file `lookups/latency_baseline.csv` with a transforms stanza) should document `cluster_name`, `vendor`, `tier_type` (hybrid, all_flash, or nvme), `read_threshold_ms`, `write_threshold_ms`, `business_service`, `owner_email`, `change_freeze_until`, and `criticality_class`. The Splunk `lookup` in Step 2 uses the cluster as the key; if a new cluster is missing, the search falls back to default tier math in the `eval` block so a blank table does not silently mean “healthy.”


Step 1 — Configure data collection

1) VMware vSAN performance poller (Heavy Forwarder or dedicated Linux collector, not on the vCenter VM). The REST surface is vSphere 7+ `POST https://<vcenter>/api/session` for a `vmware-api-session-id` (one-hour sessions are typical), then for each vSAN cluster id, `POST https://<vcenter>/api/vcenter/vsan/clusters/{cluster}/perf-query` (see VMware vCenter REST and vSAN performance documentation for the exact path on your build). The body lists entities: `cluster-domclient:*` for the cluster, `host-domclient:*` per host, `disk-group:*` per group, and `vm:*` per-VM. Flatten the JSON to one HEC line per entity per sample with the agreed contract names `iops_read`, `iops_write`, `throughput_read_mbps`, `throughput_write_mbps`, `latency_avg_read_ms`, `latency_avg_write_ms`, `latency_max_read_ms`, `latency_max_write_ms`, `congestion`, and `outstanding_io`. Sourcetype is `vmware:vsan:perfquery` (custom HEC, JSON, one line per object). The sample Python 3 poller below uses the standard library only: `ssl.create_default_context`, `urllib.request`, a corporate CA file, a session header, and explicit handling of HTTP 401 (refresh the session and retry), 429 (exponential sleep then retry with a cap), and 500 (treat as vSAN performance service or API tier unavailable and back off, still logging the failure to the poller file — do not drop silence into Splunk as “zero latency”). On success, HEC to your indexer on `services/collector/event` with the token in a secret store.

```python
import json, os, ssl, time, sys, logging, urllib.error, urllib.request

LOG = logging.getLogger("vsan_perfpoll")
if not LOG.handlers:
    h = logging.FileHandler(os.environ.get("VSAN_PERF_LOG", "/var/log/vsan_perfquery_poll.log"))
    h.setFormatter(logging.Formatter("%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(message)s"))
    LOG.addHandler(h)
    LOG.setLevel(logging.INFO)

def http_json(req, method="GET", body=None, max_retries=3):
    ctx = ssl.create_default_context(cafile=os.environ.get("SPLUNK_CA", "/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt"))
    data = None if body is None else json.dumps(body).encode("utf-8")
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        r = urllib.request.Request(req, data=data, method=method)
        for k, v in [("Content-Type", "application/json")]:
            r.add_header(k, v)
        r.add_header("vmware-api-session-id", os.environ.get("VMWARE_API_SESSION", ""))
        try:
            with urllib.request.urlopen(r, context=ctx, timeout=60) as resp:
                return json.loads(resp.read().decode("utf-8", "replace"))
        except urllib.error.HTTPError as e:
            if e.code == 401 and attempt + 1 < max_retries:
                LOG.error("vsan api 401 — renew session in outer wrapper; retrying")
                time.sleep(2 ** attempt)
                continue
            if e.code == 429 and attempt + 1 < max_retries:
                LOG.error("vsan api 429 — backing off %ss", 2 ** attempt)
                time.sleep(2 ** attempt)
                continue
            if e.code >= 500 and attempt + 1 < max_retries:
                LOG.error("vsan api %s — vSAN or API tier may be down; backoff", e.code)
                time.sleep(2 ** attempt)
                continue
            raise
    raise RuntimeError("vsan rest retry exhausted")
```

Wire your real session bootstrap, cluster list, and HEC `send` around this skeleton; keep the session renewal outside the inner GET loop if you use environment rotation.

2) `inputs.conf` and `props.conf` (Indexer or first parsing hop):

```ini
[http://vsan_hec]
disabled = 0
indexes = vmware, hci
token = <REDACTED>
useACK = 1
```

```ini
[vmware:vsan:perfquery]
TRUNCATE = 0
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
LINE_BREAKER = ([\r\n]+)
INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json
```

3) **Sampling cadence**: emit cluster-aggregate samples every thirty seconds. Schedule per-VM and `vm:*` at sixty seconds in large environments — one thousand guests once per minute is on the order of 1.44 million events per day for that slice alone, so size the index, sourcetype partition, and possibly a separate index for high-cardinality per-VM rows.

4) Nutanix: install `Splunk_TA_nutanix` (Splunkbase 5076) on a data collection tier that can reach Prism Element or Prism Central. Enable the cluster and host metrics modular inputs in `local/inputs.conf` (do not edit vendor `default`); confirm `nutanix:cluster:metrics` and `nutanix:host:metrics` land in `index=hci` (or your chosen index) with `controller_avg_io_latency_usecs` and the read and write usec fields. Latency in the Nutanix API is in microseconds; the search in Step 2 divides by one thousand to compare with millisecond SLOs.

5) Cisco HyperFlex: a second small poller (Python, same HEC style) against `https://<hx>/rest/clusters/{id}/stats?statType=ALL_DATA` (your firmware build may add query flags). Flatten to `cisco:hyperflex:cluster_perf` with `iops`, `throughput_KBps`, `read_iops`, `write_iops`, and `latency_us` in microseconds, plus `cisco:hyperflex:vm_perf` for per-VM rows. HEC the same as vSAN, different sourcetype and props stanza with JSON extraction.

6) Azure Stack HCI (optional): a scheduled task on a management or witness node running PowerShell that calls `Get-StorageQoSFlow` and, for cluster context where QoS is empty, `Get-StoragePerformanceHistory` or `Get-ClusterPerformanceHistory` depending on the Windows Server and HCI release you run. Send JSON to HEC with sourcetype `azure_stack_hci:storage_perf` and fields you normalize to the names used in the Splunk `eval` block.

7) Optional CIM: if the Splunk Add-on for VMware already maps vSphere counter streams into the Common Information Model, you may be able to run `tstats` against `datamodel=Performance` with `nodename=Performance.Storage` for normalized `latency` and `iops`. That path is not required for this file because field names and acceleration vary; treat it as an acceleration-friendly variant once your TA team confirms the dataset is populated for your vCenters.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

The canonical search below must match the `spl` field of this use case. It opens with a `comment` macro, uses `earliest=-30m@m` on each multisearch leg for short, actionable performance windows, then unifies vSAN, Nutanix, HyperFlex, and optional Azure Stack HCI in one pipeline. The `coalesce` calls absorb camelCase and snake_case. Nutanix microsecond fields are divided by one thousand in place. A `join` to a longer window supplies twenty-four hour p95 for the “trending up but still under threshold” band. Severity uses a five-bucket `case` with `AND` and `OR` in uppercase in the `eval` (Splunk is case-insensitive, but the expression reads clearly in reviews). A change freeze in the lookup demotes to `ok` on non-critical rows, but anything still classified as `critical` including max read or write over one hundred ms stays in the page even during a freeze.

**Search walkthrough** (read before paste): the multisearch arms run in parallel, then Splunk appends the rows. The first stage normalizes each vendor so `latency_avg_read_ms` is always milliseconds. The `stats` groups by `cluster_name`, `host_name`, `vendor`, and `scope` so you can read cluster, host, `disk_group`, and `vm` rows side by side; empty `host_name` is normal for pure cluster level feeds. The `lookup` enriches thresholds and owners. The join pulls twenty-five hours of history but stops thirty minutes before “now” so you are not double-counting the current bucket in both legs. The drift percentage measures how far read p95 has moved from the read threshold, which is useful for a sorted incident table. After validation, save as a report, then Save As → Alert. Schedule every three to five minutes. Throttle on `cluster_name` and `host_name` and `scope` and route by `severity_bucket`. Keep the HEC and input contracts stable — changing a field name without changing this SPL is the most common way to get an “empty, therefore healthy” panel.

```spl
`comment("UC-19.2.3 — HCI shared storage I/O read/write latency (p95, max) against per-cluster baselines. SOURCES: vmware:vsan:perfquery, nutanix:cluster:metrics, nutanix:host:metrics, cisco:hyperflex:cluster_perf, cisco:hyperflex:vm_perf, optional azure_stack_hci:storage_perf. earliest=-30m@m; change freeze demotes to ok except when severity is critical (including read_max_ms>100 OR write_max_ms>100).")`
| multisearch [
    search (index=vmware OR index=hci) sourcetype="vmware:vsan:perfquery" earliest=-30m@m
    | eval vendor="vmware"
    | eval cluster_name=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, cluster, cluster_id, clusterId, ""))
    | eval host_name=toString(coalesce(host_name, hostName, esxi_host, esxiHost, ""))
    | eval entity=lower(toString(coalesce(entity, entity_id, entityId, "")))
    | eval scope=case(like(entity, "cluster-domclient%"), "cluster", like(entity, "host-domclient%"), "host", like(entity, "disk-group%"), "disk_group", match(entity, "^vm:"), "vm", 1=1, "other")
    | eval latency_avg_read_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_avg_read_ms, latencyAvgReadMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval latency_avg_write_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_avg_write_ms, latencyAvgWriteMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval latency_max_read_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_max_read_ms, latencyMaxReadMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval latency_max_write_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_max_write_ms, latencyMaxWriteMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval iops_r=tonumber(toString(coalesce(iops_read, iopsRead, "0")), 10)
    | eval iops_w=tonumber(toString(coalesce(iops_write, iopsWrite, "0")), 10)
    | eval iops_total=iops_r+iops_w
    | table _time, cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope, latency_avg_read_ms, latency_avg_write_ms, latency_max_read_ms, latency_max_write_ms, iops_total
] [
    search (index=hci OR index=nutanix) (sourcetype="nutanix:cluster:metrics" OR sourcetype="nutanix:host:metrics") earliest=-30m@m
    | eval vendor="nutanix"
    | eval cluster_name=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name, ""))
    | eval host_name=toString(coalesce(host_name, hostName, node_name, nodeName, ""))
    | eval scope=if(sourcetype="nutanix:host:metrics", "host", "cluster")
    | eval lr=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_read_io_latency_usecs, controllerAvgReadIoLatencyUsecs, "0")), 10)/1000.0
    | eval lw=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_write_io_latency_usecs, controllerAvgWriteIoLatencyUsecs, "0")), 10)/1000.0
    | eval lcomb=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_io_latency_usecs, controllerAvgIoLatencyUsecs, "0")), 10)/1000.0
    | eval latency_avg_read_ms=if((lr>0), lr, lcomb)
    | eval latency_avg_write_ms=if((lw>0), lw, lcomb)
    | eval latency_max_read_ms=latency_avg_read_ms
    | eval latency_max_write_ms=latency_avg_write_ms
    | eval iops_total=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_num_iops, controllerNumIops, "0")), 10)
    | table _time, cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope, latency_avg_read_ms, latency_avg_write_ms, latency_max_read_ms, latency_max_write_ms, iops_total
] [
    search (index=hci OR index=cisco) (sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:cluster_perf" OR sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:vm_perf") earliest=-30m@m
    | eval vendor="cisco"
    | eval cluster_name=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, cluster_id, clusterId, ""))
    | eval host_name=toString(coalesce(host_name, hostName, vm_name, vmName, ""))
    | eval scope=if(sourcetype="cisco:hyperflex:vm_perf", "vm", "cluster")
    | eval lat_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_ms, "0")), 10)
    | eval lat_us=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_us, latencyUs, "0")), 10)
    | eval base_ms=if((lat_ms>0), lat_ms, (lat_us/1000.0))
    | eval read_i=tonumber(toString(coalesce(read_iops, readIops, "0")), 10)
    | eval write_i=tonumber(toString(coalesce(write_iops, writeIops, "0")), 10)
    | eval all_i=tonumber(toString(coalesce(iops, "0")), 10)
    | eval iops_total=if((all_i>0), all_i, (read_i+write_i))
    | eval latency_avg_read_ms=base_ms
    | eval latency_avg_write_ms=base_ms
    | eval latency_max_read_ms=base_ms
    | eval latency_max_write_ms=base_ms
    | table _time, cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope, latency_avg_read_ms, latency_avg_write_ms, latency_max_read_ms, latency_max_write_ms, iops_total
] [
    search (index=hci OR index=ashci) sourcetype="azure_stack_hci:storage_perf" earliest=-30m@m
    | eval vendor="ashci"
    | eval cluster_name=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, ClusterName, ""))
    | eval host_name=toString(coalesce(InitiatorName, initiatorName, host_name, hostName, ""))
    | eval scope="vm"
    | eval latency_avg_read_ms=tonumber(toString(coalesce(Latency, latency, "0")), 10)
    | eval latency_avg_write_ms=latency_avg_read_ms
    | eval latency_max_read_ms=latency_avg_read_ms
    | eval latency_max_write_ms=latency_avg_read_ms
    | eval iops_total=tonumber(toString(coalesce(IOPS, iops, "0")), 10)
    | table _time, cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope, latency_avg_read_ms, latency_avg_write_ms, latency_max_read_ms, latency_max_write_ms, iops_total
]
| where len(cluster_name)>0
| stats perc95(latency_avg_read_ms) AS read_p95_ms perc95(latency_avg_write_ms) AS write_p95_ms max(latency_max_read_ms) AS read_max_ms max(latency_max_write_ms) AS write_max_ms sum(iops_total) AS iops_total max(_time) AS last_event BY cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope
| lookup local=true latency_baseline cluster_name OUTPUTNEW tier_type, read_threshold_ms, write_threshold_ms, business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until, criticality_class
| eval read_base=case(tier_type="all_flash" OR match(lower(tostring(tier_type)), "all.flash"), 1, tier_type="nvme" OR match(lower(tostring(tier_type)), "nvme"), 0.5, 1=1, 5)
| eval write_base=case(tier_type="all_flash" OR match(lower(tostring(tier_type)), "all.flash"), 2, tier_type="nvme" OR match(lower(tostring(tier_type)), "nvme"), 1, 1=1, 10)
| eval read_threshold_ms=if((isnotnull(read_threshold_ms) AND (read_threshold_ms>0)), read_threshold_ms, read_base)
| eval write_threshold_ms=if((isnotnull(write_threshold_ms) AND (write_threshold_ms>0)), write_threshold_ms, write_base)
| eval tier_guess=if((read_threshold_ms<=1) AND (write_threshold_ms<=2), "likely_all_flash_or_nvme", "likely_hybrid_or_mixed")
| eval latency_drift_pct=if((read_threshold_ms>0), round(((read_p95_ms - read_threshold_ms) / read_threshold_ms) * 100, 1), null())
| join type=left max=0 cluster_name [
    search (index=vmware OR index=hci OR index=nutanix) (sourcetype="vmware:vsan:perfquery" OR sourcetype="nutanix:cluster:metrics" OR sourcetype="nutanix:host:metrics") earliest=-25h@h latest=-30m@m
    | eval c=toString(coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, cluster, name, ""))
    | eval n_r_us=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_read_io_latency_usecs, "0")), 10)
    | eval n_w_us=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_write_io_latency_usecs, "0")), 10)
    | eval n_c_us=tonumber(toString(coalesce(controller_avg_io_latency_usecs, "0")), 10)
    | eval v_r=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_avg_read_ms, latencyAvgReadMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval v_w=tonumber(toString(coalesce(latency_avg_write_ms, latencyAvgWriteMs, "0")), 10)
    | eval r_t=if(n_r_us>0, n_r_us/1000, if(n_c_us>0, n_c_us/1000, v_r))
    | eval w_t=if(n_w_us>0, n_w_us/1000, if(n_c_us>0, n_c_us/1000, v_w))
    | where len(c)>0
    | stats perc95(r_t) AS read_p95_24h perc95(w_t) AS write_p95_24h BY c
    | rename c AS cluster_name
]
| eval trend_up=if((isnotnull(read_p95_24h) OR isnotnull(write_p95_24h)) AND (read_p95_ms <= read_threshold_ms) AND (write_p95_ms <= write_threshold_ms) AND (((read_p95_24h>0) AND (read_p95_ms > 1.1*read_p95_24h)) OR ((write_p95_24h>0) AND (write_p95_ms > 1.1*write_p95_24h))), 1, 0)
| eval severity_bucket=case((read_p95_ms > 4*read_threshold_ms) OR (write_p95_ms > 4*write_threshold_ms) OR (read_max_ms > 100) OR (write_max_ms > 100), "critical", (read_p95_ms > 2*read_threshold_ms) OR (write_p95_ms > 2*write_threshold_ms), "high", (read_p95_ms > read_threshold_ms) OR (write_p95_ms > write_threshold_ms), "medium", (trend_up=1), "low", 1=1, "ok")
| eval ft=coalesce(strptime(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"), strptime(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, "")), "%Y-%m-%d"))
| eval freeze_active=if((isnotnull(change_freeze_until) AND (len(trim(toString(coalesce(change_freeze_until, ""))))>0) AND (now() < coalesce(ft, 0))), 1, 0)
| eval severity_bucket=if((freeze_active=1) AND (severity_bucket!="critical"), "ok", severity_bucket)
| eval sev_order=case(severity_bucket="critical", 1, severity_bucket="high", 2, severity_bucket="medium", 3, severity_bucket="low", 4, 1=1, 5)
| sort 0 sev_order, -latency_drift_pct, cluster_name, scope, host_name
| table last_event, cluster_name, host_name, vendor, scope, tier_type, tier_guess, iops_total, read_p95_ms, write_p95_ms, read_max_ms, write_max_ms, read_threshold_ms, write_threshold_ms, read_p95_24h, write_p95_24h, trend_up, latency_drift_pct, severity_bucket, freeze_active, business_service, owner_email, criticality_class
```

Optional `tstats` for environments with `Performance.Storage` accelerated:

```spl
| tstats prestats=false summariesonly=true local=true
    avg(Performance.Storage.latency) AS mlat
    sum(Performance.Storage.iops) AS miops
  from datamodel=Performance
  where nodename=Performance.Storage
  by _time, Performance.name span=1m
| eval latency_ms=round(mlat, 2)
| timechart span=5m p95(latency_ms) as p95_lat by Performance.name
```

You must have field aliases in place so `Performance.Storage.latency` is in milliseconds, not a raw vSphere counter number; validate against the Data Model preview in Settings before you trust this for paging.

Step 3 — Validate

1) VMware vSphere Client: open the vSAN cluster, Monitor → vSAN → Performance, then Backend, Disks, or virtual machine tabs depending on whether you are proving cluster, disk-group, or per-VM feeds. Check that the UI’s latency trend lines and order of magnitude match the newest `vmware:vsan:perfquery` event for the same object id within a couple of minutes of poll skew.

2) Nutanix Prism: Home → select the cluster → Performance → Latency widget and read path versus controller latency. Compare the direction of a spike, not a digit-perfect match, because Prism may average on a different interval than your Splunk `span` or `stats` pass.

3) HyperFlex: in HX Connect, open Performance and read Cluster I/O Latency, then a VM with known hot storage if you are validating `cisco:hyperflex:vm_perf`.

4) Windows Admin Center: for Azure Stack HCI, Cluster → Performance → Storage, compare queue depth and latency where your JSON feed names align.

5) ESXi host: `esxcli vsan storage list` to confirm which disk groups and devices map to a hot `disk-group:*` in Splunk. `esxcli vsan debug evacuation` (or the evacuation-related debug verbs your vSphere build documents) and other `esxcli vsan debug` subcommands can show resync and evacuation context in some releases—use the VMware reference for your exact server version, not a third-party paraphrase. Quote high-level results to the change record, not raw device identifiers, in uncontrolled email.

6) Nutanix `ncli` on a CVM, for example `ncli sp ls-stats` or the statistics family your runbook already uses for a second opinion on controller and Stargate latency when Splunk and Prism differ.

7) REST checks (lab only, rotate secrets): vCenter `GET /api/vcenter/vsan/clusters` then `.../perf-query` with a session header; Nutanix `GET https://<prism>:9440/PrismGateway/services/rest/v2.0/cluster` and metrics endpoints; HyperFlex `GET /rest/clusters` then `/stats`. Compare JSON fields to HEC.

8) If Splunk disagrees with a console: check sampling interval (thirty vs sixty seconds), whether the console shows average and Splunk shows p95, and whether a per-VM hot spot is hidden inside a clean cluster number — that is an expected find for this use case, not a product bug on either side.

Step 4 — Operationalize

Dashboards: Row one — two single value tiles, “count of clusters with read_p95_ms greater than two times the read threshold” and “count of clusters with `severity_bucket` equal to `critical`.” Row two — a sortable table of the full `table` in the search, default sort on `latency_drift_pct` descending. Row three — a timechart of `read_p95_ms` and `write_p95_ms` by `cluster_name` for the last four hours, time picker on short windows. Row four — a scatter of `iops_total` on the x-axis and `read_p95_ms` on the y-axis, split by `vendor` or `tier_type`, to find the “knee” where latency rises with offered load.

Alerting: PagerDuty on `critical` with cluster, vendor, and owner. ServiceNow P2 for `high` with a link to the runbook. Slack for `medium` to a low-noise room with chart attachments. Email digests for `low` and for `ok` if you also run a “everything fine” report for audit. Throttle and dedupe on `(cluster_name, host_name, scope)`.

ITSI, if you use it: one KPI per cluster for `read_p95_ms`, one for `write_p95_ms`, and one for `latency_drift_pct` with a glass table that shows each three next to a service from `business_service` in the lookup. Keep episode titles in plain language, not all caps.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

(a) Empty result table — confirm HEC tokens and the Heavy Forwarder can reach 443 to vCenter, Prism, or HX. vSphere session tokens often expire in about an hour; renew in the poller, not in Splunk. In vCenter, vSAN → Configure → Performance Service must be enabled, or the perf-query body returns an empty time series and every downstream panel is empty.

(b) Latency in wrong units — if Nutanix or HyperFlex numbers look one thousand times too large, you are still in microseconds in one branch. The coalesce in Step 2 divides Nutanix usec fields by a thousand. Repeat the check for a `_usecs` suffix on any new field a TA adds in an upgrade.

(c) Per-VM vSAN is missing — the `vm:*` path requires vSAN VM-level performance collection enabled in vCenter for those objects, and a poll interval you can afford. You will only see per-VM rows when the poller enqueues those entities in the request body and vCenter returns them.

(d) Cluster is “ok” but an app is slow — the cluster aggregate is an average. Split by `scope=vm` and reread `read_max_ms`. Snapshot storms and single-VM misplacement are classic causes.

(e) Percentile feels off — at thirty second samples in a thirty minute window, you have about sixty samples, enough for a stable p95. If an indexer only ingested a partial window, `perc95` is noisy; widen `earliest` to `-60m` for investigations only, not the default SLO.

(f) Freeze demoted a row you still need as critical — confirm the `eval` only demotes when `freeze_active=1` and `severity_bucket` is not `critical`. If a row that should be critical still shows as `ok`, a typo in the not-equal to critical test is the first place to look, then verify `strptime` on `change_freeze_until`.

(g) HyperFlex latency looks like raw microseconds in one panel — treat `latency_us` the same as Nutanix: convert to ms before you join to thresholds.

(h) Azure `Get-StorageQoSFlow` is empty — that cmdlet only returns flows with an active Storage QoS policy. For a cluster without QoS, call `Get-StoragePerformanceHistory` or `Get-ClusterPerformanceHistory` in your runbooked pattern and HEC a reduced schema that still carries average latency in milliseconds for that cluster object.

(i) Cache hit ratio — vSAN can expose a cache hit rate and Nutanix an oplog hit rate. If you add those fields in the HEC, extend the `case` so “low hit plus high latency” can bump severity one notch because it usually means a capacity-tier miss storm.

(j) Baseline lookup is empty — the search falls back to about five and ten millisecond read and write defaults for hybrid, one and two for all-flash, and half and one for NVMe, via the `read_base` and `write_base` expressions. Re-populate the CSV for production governance.

(k) NetApp HCI, Dell VxFlex OS, or PowerStore in “HCI mode” — you still need a vendor’s REST or ONTAP/Element metrics feed into Splunk and the same p95 versus tier pattern; map their latency fields in a fifth multisearch block once you have stable sourcetypes. This JSON does not ship those field names because they are site-specific.
grandmaExplanation: We track how long storage reads and writes take across your hyperconverged clusters, and we separate a big IOPS number from a bad latency number so we catch slowdowns that capacity charts miss. We show the worst and the typical delay side by side, route the right team when it crosses the line, and point them to the cluster, node, or disk group to fix.
hardware: Applies to any VMware vSAN OSA/ESA on ReadyNodes and OEM appliances, Nutanix NX and other Nutanix-qualified platforms under AOS, Cisco HX-Series hyperconverged nodes, and Windows Server Azure Stack HCI clusters where Storage Spaces Direct performance history or QoS flow exports are available. Controllers, NICs, NVMe, and cache-tier flash must match the vendor HCL for the release you run; a mis-matched HBA or driver can raise latency with no change in the logical capacity the capacity use cases read. ESA and NVMe designs often sit a full order under hybrid latency — align `tier_type` in the lookup with what you actually purchased.
